speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is December 1, 1995. The survivor is Zora Goldberger. Her maiden name was Ferber. I'm Elizabeth Yonan. This interview is taking place in Chicago, Illinois, in the USA. And the language we're using is English. OK, I'm Elizabeth Yonan, and we're conducting the interview with Zora Goldberger. Today is December 1, 1995. And this interview is being conducted in Chicago, Illinois, in English. Could you tell me your name and also spell it for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Zora Goldberger, Z-O-R-A G-O-L-D-B-E-R-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth name is F-E-R-- F-E-R-B-E-R, Ferber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what year you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: November 5, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-- date-- in the Podravska Slatina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-O-D-R-- capital S-L-A-T-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in Yugoslavia, ex-Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- well, it was a very small town. Very small town. Maybe 6,000 people, but very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beautiful town. We had wonderful time there. And I just loved it. I had a very nice childhood. And I don't know what you want to know about, but it was nice. My parents were very lovely people. I had two sister, one older and one younger, and younger brother. And my grandparents were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit more about your family. Could you tell me your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' name? My father's name is Albert Ferber, A-L-B-E-R-T, Albert. And my mother Elizabet, [LAUGHS]. E-L-I-Z-I-- Liza-- no. A. Liza. B-E-T. OK, Elizabet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your parents-- did your parents do anything for a living? What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- we had a hotel. My parents had a hotel in Slatina, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hotel Slatina. That was the name, Hotel Slatina. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers and sisters, can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister was Margita, M-A-R-G-I-T-A, Margita. And my brother's name was Mirko, M-I-R-K-O. And my younger sister was Vera, V-E-R-A. She was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I want to go back to your brothers and sisters. What year were they born, like your oldest sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My--my older sister, yeah. And then me, and my brother, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the years that they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it was four years between us. So it should be-- I was-- my older sister was '14. And my brother was '22, 1922. And the younger sister '26.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. Is there a particularly favorite memory that you have of your brother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite memory. We were very close, very close. My older sister-- we were all different. I was more outgoing. My older sister was a little bit on shy. And when we were very young, we didn't get along. She didn't-- not that she didn't like me, but maybe she was a little bit jealous because I was very affectionate with my mother. I loved my mother. And she-- she loved all the children the same, I'm sure of that. But somehow I was her favorite, because I was very affectionate. And I always did small things for her, for family. And my younger sister, she was so beautiful, and I loved her. And my brother-- I have a good memory of him, but when he was about 14 years old, he went to the school. We didn't have a high school. We had a high school in-- in Slatina, but he went in a bigger-- like a college.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I remember we got a letter to my parents that, what happened to my brother, to Mirko? He wasn't in the school for a month. And he went every day with a train to the city, Virovitica, what is the name. And my mother went to the school. My God, he went with the boys and played cards. He

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't-- And he was expelled, not only for this. For every school he was expelled. And never my father-- I never see any of us beaten. But when he came home, he was beaten from my father. I remember I kneeled. I says, Father, please, enough. Don't-- don't do that anymore. And that was a big tragedy for us, because the only boy, and he couldn't go to school. No school would accept him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back a little bit further to when you were very young, say under age 10, and talk about what you did with your sisters and your brother. I mean, what games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: What game did we play?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We played gin rummy. [LAUGHS] And what play did we play? We went gymnastic. You know. I wasn't too popular for that, but my older sister was very popular. And my younger sister was very young then. You know, later, she was-- before I got married, she was bigger, and we have more in common than when she was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about, say, when you were small, the typical day in your house. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] What was that. You know, it-- it-- it's hard to say, because like I said, we had hotel. They didn't like us to be too much and we-- we were in our room. And we traveled, you know, all the time. They send us to-- to aunts in other city, and Adriatic Sea. So I-- during the vacation, I was very-- not too much at home. You know? And when I was young, I-- I-- I wanted to dance. I was to be-- want to be ballerina. And I remember I always-- when my mother had a company, I was always dancing for them. And-- but they didn't let me pursue that. And I finished school. And I didn't do anything till I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to still talk a little bit more about your childhood, too, and your town. Tell me about school. How much education did you have, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a high school, and I have two years of junior college. That's all I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were young and in school, what was your favorite subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite subject was mathematic and reading. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't like too much the school, telling the truth. I didn't like too much when I was-- now I'm sorry about it. But when I was young, I-- I was like a little butterfly, always wanted to-- to do something and going places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I think now, I wasn't too serious. I always liked it to dress up and-- and just go to one party to the other party, dancing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite dress that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I have a blue-- I remember, very beautiful blue dress. That was my favorite dress. And then later, I remember I got a white dress. And everybody says, oh, it's so pretty. And I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you quite popular as a young person?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very popular when I was young. I had lots of boyfriends and lots of girlfriends. I was very popular.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. Tell me about some of your friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had two of my best girlfriend. And really my best girlfriend, one was Croatian, and the other one was Serbian. And we were inseparable. You know? And that's why it's so hard for me later to believe what happened, because I know I was born Jewish, but that's all. We didn't have any-- any-- anything, you know, prejudice, like they have here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were all always together. On Christmas, I went to their houses, you know, and even in church with them. And they did the same when we had our high holiday. And that's something what I will remember all my life. They were wonderful friends, and they were friend till the last moment. They really helped me escape. You know? Was it quite commonplace in-- in the town that you grew up in for Jews and Gentiles to mix?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- I-- I didn't know any difference. We didn't know any difference. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: About what percentage of the population in the town you grew up in was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe not even 100 people. You know. It was a small community. We had our synagogue. And on high-- high holiday, I went there, because we all got dressed, and-- and we went. You know, and the family came. All the family from the other city, they always came to ours, because like I said, we had hotel. And we always had a nice reunion. And that, I miss still now. Whenever holidays come, I miss that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious background? Were you observant, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My religious-- not too much unfortunately. Not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, not too much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I-- I was a good-- I know I was Jewish, and-- but we were not raised too religiously. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, and even Hanukkah. Yeah. I remember we did have a Hanukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanukkah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we had the candles. And I remember we got some gifts. And somehow that was my favorite. I don't know why, but it was my favorite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your favorite gift that you got for Hanukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. What-- we-- we-- we didn't have too much. Candies, things like that. We didn't have-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family celebrate Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did they? I don't remember that we-- we-- we did. Sometimes I went to-- to synagogue on Shabbat with some other friends. I did. Somehow, I-- sometime I had a feeling I-- I wanted to go. I wanted to go. But somehow, I didn't. Not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Did you have any type of religious schooling at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have that. We-- we have religious classes, because everybody has to have, you know, when you are in school, for one hour once a week. But nothing that I remember too much about it. I remember our rabbi, he did that. And he taught us a little bit of Hebrew. And I still remember the-- what he says, like-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] now I-- now I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, that was the only thing what I can remember about religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Religious school. Do you remember your rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Green-- Greenwald. I know he was Hungarian. And he was very fat. And he always says, oh, Zora. Because I was always going, walking. And he says, Zora, you always walking. Nothing is going to be from you, because you-- you are not too serious. If he would see me now, he wouldn't believe it that I am serious, and I learned lot in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were kind of a dreamer when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dreamer. Big dreamer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you were very, very close to your mother. Can you describe a little bit about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother. My mother, she-- she was very beautiful woman. I don't know if she was too happy in her life. I don't think she was too happy. My father was a very nice guy, but very quiet. And my mother was much more intelligent than my father. You know? But she was very affectionate, and wonderful, wonderful mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about her.

speaker: interviewee
text: About her?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name-- maiden name was Krausz. K-R-A-U-S-Z, Krausz. And I-- I-- I remember her-- her parents were living in-- in Slatina. I loved my grandfather. My grandmother, she didn't-- somehow, she loved my older sister. She liked me, but she always said, ah, nothing is going to happen to Zora. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't want-- I didn't want to do anything in the house. Not that I needed, because we had help. You know? But my older sister, she went in the kitchen, and she learned how to cook. I never did. I-- I didn't have any desire for it. And they thought that I'm going to be all the time like that, but I changed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't have too much to tell about my-- my childhood. Not too much. Not too much. I was happy-- in-- in beginning, I was very happy, I remember. But then later, something happened with my father. He lost lots of money on some speculation, and we had a little bit rough time. And he blamed my mother, because he thought that my mother spoil us too much, give us everything. And I remember one day-- I don't want to remember, but I remember. One day, I came in the bedroom, and I saw him with a knife with the mother. And I remember. I says, oh, Father. Please don't do that. I-- I-- I took the knife and I started to cry, and I fainted. I-- I remember that. That's the only bad thing I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, everything was OK. That's the only incident that I remember. But for long, long time, I just couldn't get out of my system when I saw that. You know? I don't know why he did it, but maybe they had a fight about financial situation. That I remember. But then later, he changed. Then got a little better. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a normal-- normal life. I want to go now-- let's say you're a little bit older, say, a teenager. And you're going to gymnasium. Or did they call it gymnasium in Yugoslavia also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like for you? Describe your teenage years.

speaker: interviewee
text: How was it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, being a teenager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I-- I was a happy-go-lucky teenager. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, it was different then in-- in-- in school than here. It's completely different. You know? I went in school four hours. Then we came home. I had my homework done, what I didn't like too much. [LAUGHS] I don't like to talk about, because I-- I really didn't like school too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other activities that you took place in? I know you liked to dance. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dance. Dance. Dance. I liked to dance. I even won once a-- a contest, you know, for for Hungarian Csardas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. What is Csardas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Csardas? That's a Hungarian folk dance. Csardas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take lessons, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just picked it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I just picked it up, yeah. Because sometimes-- we had hotel, but we have night-- nightclub too, you know. In the night, we have the music, and people came and danced. And I remember, even if they didn't let us come, but I sneaked and I watched. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about like what it was like to grow up in a-- in a hotel, and what type of guests came, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of guests?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: People who travel, you know. They came over the night. Those kind of people, you know. They came. And then we-- for dinners, you know, we have some steady guests. My mother's sister, who was a widow, she was cook. She cooked, and she was a wonderful cook. And we had steady customers just to come for dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother cook at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw her in the kitchen, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. I always saw her dressed up, and-- and be a big lady. No, she never did anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your aunt did all the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt did all the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did all the cooking, and she-- we have other help, you know, who help her. But my aunt, she was cooking. Yeah. She was cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your favorite dish that she made?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite? Hungarian goulash and chicken paprikash. Yeah. That was my favorite food then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the guests that stayed at the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I-- I remember once, Minister of Agriculture came from Belgrade through-- through Slatina. And he stayed in our hotel. And that was a big sensation. That, I remember. But otherwise, just ordinary people, you know, who came business reason. And they stayed for over the night, or maybe two, three nights. That's all what-- what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you'd like to mention about growing up and being young in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] What should-- what should I remember? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What should I remember. How-- how was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of your favorite memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Favor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we have every-- [LAUGHS]. You have to laugh. Every Saturday evening and Sunday afternoon, we went to the other place for dancing. You know. We-- we had all-- every Saturday night, young people went dancing. Chaperoned, naturally. My mother always-- we never could go without. You know, it's different, like now. And that I remember. That was a beautiful time. Beautiful time. And we went to movies a lot. And sometimes they came from the big city, concert. And I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the operas, they came. I-- I remember, it was-- what is the name of the opera-- and I was in it, too. They asked children to be in it. [NON-ENGLISH] was the name. I don't know how to say it in-- in English. That I remember. It was fascinating. Yeah. I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reciting something. I still remember what-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you translate it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a little mouse. [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know anything else, only health and happiness. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you were in this opera?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was maybe eight, nine years old. And I still remember my favorite teacher in-- in-- she was a wonderful lady. And she has a little boy who was three years younger than I am. And I was always picked up from school to go to her house to play with him. And when she had a birthday, she always have a party. All the teachers came. And I was always invited for this party from the school. Bring her the flower, and tell her happy-- happy birthday. I remember that. Every year, it was like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name? Schuls. S-C-H-U-L-S, Schuls. I-- Mary Schuls. M-A-R-Y, Mary Schuls. I remember her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember her son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her son's name that you used to play with.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you used to play with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, eight years old till-- till 10 years old. From seven till 10, every year I-- I did that, I went to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of games did you play? Did you play outdoors or indoors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outdoors. Outdoors, with-- with balls. You know what children play with the balls, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like they play here. What's the name what they playing here? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] See, now I cannot remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Can you describe it for me? Maybe I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the game for me? Maybe I'll know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the children go around, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they are singing. And they are calling the names. You are a queen, I want the queen, something like that. It's hard to translate. You know, certain things is hard to translate. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like fun, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's-- it's-- it's hard to-- I-- probably later, I'm going to remember it, but right now I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you remember, you can tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I cannot recall now anything else, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing else I can recall, no. No. Nothing. I-- I know when I was 18 years old, I went for a while to Zagreb, what is the capital now in-- in Croatia. I had an aunt there. And oh, I had a wonderful time there. I-- I was there I don't know how many months. I went to every opera, and just had a wonderful time, made lots of friends there. I-- I liked to travel. Oh, I remember when I was I think 13 years old, I got pneumonia. And they send me to Slavonia. Slavonia is like here, Aspen, you know, to recuperate. And I was there three months. And that, I remember all my life. That was-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. That's like a-- like a camp, you know, for young girls. And oh, we had wonderful time. And I remember our-- we called her Aunt, the one who was director of this camp. One day, she had to go to a baroness, because it was a castle there. And she needed the gloves. So we make a-- a nice poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] goes to baroness, and she-- it rhymes in-- in-- you know. I cannot. But it was so funny. And I-- I remember the nurse, she always came. We had to had [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The children, you know, you have to go to washroom to have a-- how do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Like a cough medicine. It's a cough medicine. And I remember a song. We said, sister, knock, knock, knock. Sister comes in room, asks you for-- to take the medicine. That I remember, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a picture from this. We were I don't know how many people there. And we had lots of parties, children parties, you know, of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three months. That was in summer. That's a mountain. You know. And when I came back, I-- I was OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. What year was that? '18, '28-- in '30s. 1930, yeah. OK?
